How does the maximum detachment force required to remove a plastic disk

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are wellâlabelled, include units and uncertainties, and feature clear headings.
Processed calculations (areas, force differences, averages) are logically presented and mathematically sound.
The scatter-plot with best-fit line, error bars and RÂ² value demonstrates precise data visualization.
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation is applied to only one data point and omitted from the final gradient uncertainty.
Some raw data appear miscalibrated (e.g. 0.000 N initial reading) without explanation of zero-offset handling.
The reported range of uncertainties (â2â13%â) is not linked to individual points or discussed in trend interpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores: Communication 4, Uncertainties 3, Processing quality 3. Communication is clear and precise (band-4), while consideration of uncertainties and completeness of processing sit in band-3 due to omissions. Overall this best fits a band-4 performance (4/6).

strengths: The research question is situated in a specific and authentic context (paintâcan lids, waterâstrider locomotion), making the studyâs relevance unambiguous.
A detailed stepâbyâstep procedure, complete materials list (with dimensions) and clear variable table are provided, supporting largely replicable methodology.
Variables are systematically categorized with descriptions of measurement methods and uncertainties, underlining reproducibility.
weaknesses: Temperature control lacks specified tolerances and monitoring frequency, limiting methodological rigour.
Theoretical links (e.g. contact angle, immersion depth) are not fully developed; key assumptions in simplifying the force equation are unexplained.
No schematic of the experimental setup is included, which would aid clarity and reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores: Context 5â6, Methodology description 3â4, Methodological considerations 3â4. The research question context meets the top band, but the methodology and consideration strands fall in the 3â4 band (minor ambiguities, limited explanations). Overall this aligns with a band-4 performance (4/6).

strengths: The conclusion restates the research question and cites specific numerical evidence (max force values, RÂ², gradient), ensuring full consistency with analysis.
Key findings are clearly articulated and justified by the processed data.
weaknesses: Comparison to theoretical models remains qualitative; no quantitative assessment of agreement between experimental and theoretical gradients.
The scientific context comparison lacks depth, limiting justification in the accepted scientific framework.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores: Relevance/support 5, Scientific context 3. The justified conclusion meets the highest band, but the comparison to scientific context is only in band-3. This places the overall conclusion in band-4 (4/6).

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are described (pull angle, zero-offset, temperature effects) with discussion of their impact.
Realistic improvements are suggested for each identified weakness (vertical guide, better temperature control, calibrated balances).
weaknesses: Improvements are described rather than explained with quantitative estimates of how they would reduce uncertainties.
Recommendations (e.g. cross-calibrating multiple spring balances) lack a detailed plan to ensure consistent systematic error control.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores: Weaknesses 4, Improvements 3. The student explains specific methodological limitations (band-4) and proposes realistic improvements (band-3). Together this aligns with an overall band-4 performance (4/6).

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly articulated and anchored in specific, authentic situations (paint can lids, water-strider locomotion).  These concrete examples supply a wellâdefined physical context and make the aim and relevance of the study unambiguous, satisfying the descriptor for a âspecific and appropriate context.â

Instrumental uncertainties are stated; percentage uncertainties for each data point and gradient range are included.  Propagation to the final relationship and discussion of combined uncertainty are missing, so there are âsome significant omissions.â

Relevant considerations (temperature, string length, multiple trials, radius range) are described, and six radii with five repeats yield sufficient data.  However the rationale for the chosen radius range and trial number is not explained, and some controls (water depth, disk mass) are only mentioned after the fact.  This aligns with the âdescribedâ level rather than âexplained.â

Several specific weaknesses (pull angle, temperature, vacuum, zero error, multiple balances) are described and their likely impacts (random vs systematic, magnitude) are discussed in narrative form, fulfilling the descriptor for band 3-4.

A step-by-step procedure, materials list (with dimensions) and variable table are given.  An experienced peer could repeat the study with only minor clarifications (e.g. water depth, disk thickness).  Thus replication is largely possible but not flawless, matching the 3-4 band.

Averages, areas and percentage uncertainties are calculated correctly, and a linear fit is obtained.  Minor arithmetic slips (e.g. average for 5 cm disk) and lack of uncertainty in gradient show âsome inaccuracies,â so the middle band is appropriate.

For each weakness, a realistic improvement is offered (vertical guide, temperature control, stiffer material, calibrated balance), but explanations of how the suggestions would quantitatively enhance reliability are brief.  The improvements are âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplained.â

Raw and processed data are organised in tables with units, uncertainties and clear headings; a labelled scatter-plot with best-fit and error bars is provided.  Formatting glitches exist but do not impede clarity, so communication is largely both clear and precise.

The experimentally determined trend is compared with a published theoretical equation, but the comparison remains qualitative and does not quantify agreement or discuss discrepancies.  This constitutes âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than a fully justified one.

The student states a clear relationship, cites numerical evidence (highest F, RÂ²=0.996, gradient) and ties it directly to the processed graph; the conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis presented.

annotations.comment: The evaluation section correctly differentiates between random and systematic errors (pull angle, temperature, zero offset), showing clear identification of methodological weaknesses.
When addressing zeroâoffset error, propose using a spring balance with tare functionality or calibrating the scale before each trial, and document calibration readings.
The recommendation to use the same spring balance per disk size is realistic but lacks a plan for crossâcalibrating multiple devices to ensure consistent systematic error control.

The introduction effectively anchors the research question in authentic contexts (paint can lids, water strider locomotion), providing a specific and appropriate physical context that clarifies the studyâs relevance.

The background theory defines surface tension well but does not explicitly link these concepts to experimental variables (e.g., contact angle, immersion depth). Consider elaborating how theory informs variable selection and expected trends.
The theoretical equation $$F_{max}=\pi r^2 Z\rho g +2\pi r\gamma\sin\theta$$ is cited without derivation or discussion of how each term applies under your experimental conditions.
Clarify the assumptions used to simplify to $$F=L\times T=2\pi rT$$ (e.g. negligible immersion depth, constant contact angle) to justify using this relation in your analysis.
Variables are systematically categorized with clear descriptions of how each will be measured, including instrumental uncertainties, supporting reproducibility of the investigation.

Temperature control is only described as âaround 25 Â°Câ without specifying measurement tolerance or monitoring frequency. Provide thermometer readings with uncertainties to strengthen control of this variable.
The procedure notes a fixed string length but does not describe how the stringâs mass impact was measured or subtracted. Could the stringâs weight introduce bias in the force readings?
Including a labeled schematic of the experimental setup (water depth, needle position, pull guide) would greatly aid reproducibility and clarify spatial arrangements.
The safety section demonstrates good lab practice and clearly communicates risk mitigation, enhancing the methodological rigor.
The description of initial force in results is vague. Specify how the floating equilibrium reading is determined and distinguished from pre-loading of the spring scale to avoid ambiguity.
Raw data show 0.000 N initial readings for the 10 cm disk, which likely reflect miscalibration. Validate these values or explain how zero offset was handled.
Include a summary of average uncertainties alongside each processed data point to enable clear evaluation of precision across measurements.
Calculations of area, force difference, and average force using $$A=\pi r^2$$ and $$F=F_{final}-F_{initial}$$ are sound and clearly presented.
Error propagation is demonstrated for one case but isnât applied to all data points or carried through to the final gradient uncertainty. Extend this to the full dataset.
The processed data table effectively presents percentage uncertainties for both area and force, aiding in transparent assessment of measurement precision.
The high percent uncertainty (13%) at A=310 cmÂ² is not discussed. Explain whether this outlier was weighted differently or how it affects confidence in the linear fit.
The graph presents a clear linear trend with well-labelled axes, error bars, and an RÂ² value near unity, demonstrating precise data visualization.
Text reports â2â13%â uncertainty but fails to link these values to individual data points or discuss how this range affects trend interpretation. Add that analysis.
The conclusion succinctly restates the research question and cites numerical evidence (max force, RÂ², gradient), ensuring the conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis.
Comparison to theoretical models is qualitative. Quantify the agreement between your experimental gradient and the theoretical prediction to strengthen scientific context comparison.
The evaluation identifies key strengths (clear trend, multiple trials, precise equipment) demonstrating appropriate methodological reflection.
Improvements are suggested (e.g., vertical guide, temperature monitoring) but lack quantitative estimation of how each change would reduce uncertainty. Explain expected impact of each improvement.
Consider performing a statistical test (e.g., paired t-test) on repeated trials to quantify the significance of measurement differences and validate suggested improvements.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
